topVideos.title: Why Blue Crabs Have Glowing Eyes #Shorts

bestRetention.title: Why Blue Crabs Have Glowing Eyes #Shorts
bestRetention.note: Best retention in window — nearly 2x the 19-25s median. The 'Why [animal] [sensory surprise]' hook holds viewers.

titleLessons: 'Why [animal] [surprising sensory fact] #Shorts' is the winning frame — best views AND best retention (blue crabs, 38s).
'The [animal] that can [surprising ability] #Shorts' and 'This [animal] [surprising trait] #Shorts' are solid view-drivers (590-742) but retain ~19-25s.
Documentary-style title frames ('Octopus Maternal Sacrifice...', 'Tardigrades: The Microscopic Survivors') pull views but tank retention (13s, flagged optimize). Avoid dry/documentary titles.
EXP-001 (title test on fj4-WKURK24) is still running — not yet evaluated. No title lesson from it yet.
basedOn.patternInsight: Animal SENSES and SUPERPOWERS with a curiosity-gap 'Why...' or 'The...that can...' hook win. Sensory surprises (glowing eyes) retain best. Avoid documentary-tone titles.

ideas.title: Why the Owl Hunts in Total Silence #Shorts
ideas.concept: Owls have comb-like serrations on their primary feathers plus a velvety surface that breaks turbulence into micro-currents, making their flight nearly silent — and asymmetric ear openings let them hunt in 3D sound.
ideas.hook: An owl can fly right past its prey — and the prey never hears a thing. Here's how.
ideas.keyVisual: Slow-motion barn owl gliding silently over a moonlit meadow, then a macro close-up of the feather edge serrations.

ideas.title: The Frog That Freezes Solid and Wakes Up #Shorts
ideas.concept: Wood frogs survive winter frozen solid — heart stopped, brain flatlined — because their livers flood their cells with glucose that acts as antifreeze. In spring they thaw and hop away.
ideas.hook: This frog lets its whole body freeze solid — heart, brain, everything — then wakes up in spring.
ideas.keyVisual: A wood frog encased in ice in a frozen forest floor, then the same frog thawing and blinking awake as snow melts.

ideas.title: Why the Axolotl Can Regrow Its Own Heart #Shorts
ideas.concept: Axolotls regenerate entire limbs, spinal cords, parts of their heart and brain — without scarring. Scientists study them for clues to human regenerative medicine.
ideas.hook: This animal can regrow its own heart. And its brain. And its limbs. How?
ideas.keyVisual: An axolotl in a clear tank, pale pink with feathery gills, with a close-up of a regenerating limb bud.
